gold.rationale: The article’s main claim is supported by the cited source material and the reused product images. Pricing, product variants, physical specs, battery, durability, common use cases, and Xiaomi ecosystem positioning are all consistent with the available evidence.

retrieved_evidence.text:
#小米Tag# Xiaomi Tag/Xiaomi Tag UWB 现已开售 
 
BLE 蓝牙版本定价 69 元，UWB 版本定价为 129 元。重量仅 10 克，厚度 7.2 毫米，小巧轻薄，支持 IP67 级防尘防水，内置 CR2032 纽扣电池，官方标称续航可达 1 年。
 
在使用场景上，用户可将小米 Tag 放入钱包、手提包，或悬挂在钥匙、行李箱等物品 ​​​
#小米Tag追踪器售价69元起# 69元起的小米Tag，这价格真的不讲武德了！支持蓝牙/UWB，还兼容苹果Find My，钥匙、背包、行李箱全能挂。大家觉得怎么样？ ​​​

#小米Tag# 国行上架
普通版本售价69/个，239/4个
UWB版本129/个，396/4个
这价格怎么样？

#小米Tag追踪器售价69元起#
#小米Tag售价69元起# ​​​

#小米澎湃OS3# “查找设备”已同步支持#小米Tag# [举手] 

#XiaomiTag# ​​​​
#小米Tag追踪器售价69元起# ​​​

Early marketing visuals shared by Xiaomi fans in recent days illustrate a range of suggested scenarios for the use of the new Xiaomi Tag, including attachment to keys, handbags, wallets and luggage. The examples are presented as typical everyday situations in which users may wish to locate misplaced items quickly. Retail analysts say such imagery is intended to familiarise potential buyers with the concept of item‑tracking accessories, a segment that has been growing steadily in major Chinese cities.

#小米Tag追踪器售价69元起#
兼容安卓和苹果，双持用户福音
外观设计看起来很不错，像一个精美小挂饰
AirTag完美替代，主要是便宜呀
69/249，不到三折 ​​​

On Xiaomi Mall, the company’s official online retail platform in China, the Xiaomi Tag has appeared in standard product listings that specify pricing, basic specifications and purchasing options. One listing widely discussed on Weibo shows the standard Xiaomi Tag offered at 69 yuan, with details such as stock availability, supported Xiaomi devices and delivery estimates. According to commenters citing this listing, the entry-level model focuses on Bluetooth-based tracking within the Xiaomi device-finding ecosystem, targeting users seeking an affordable way to keep track of frequently misplaced belongings.

In addition to the basic model, Xiaomi is preparing a higher-end Xiaomi Tag variant featuring ultra‑wideband technology, with a unit price of 129 yuan according to materials cited by online commentators this week. The UWB version is presented as the premium option in the new line-up and is expected to sit above the standard tracker in the domestic price structure. Retail channels in China are reportedly making initial preparations for listing both versions, but detailed launch schedules remain subject to confirmation.

Everyday usage scenarios promoted by Xiaomi focus on how the Xiaomi Tag can be attached to common personal items using keyrings, straps or simply by placing the device alongside valuables. Marketing materials highlight examples such as fastening the tracker to a backpack loop, keeping it on a keychain, or slipping it into a wallet pocket to safeguard cash and cards. According to Xiaomi’s product team, these use cases are intended to demonstrate the practicality of a small, lightweight locator tied into the Xiaomi device-finding ecosystem and, for some configurations, advertised compatibility with Apple’s Find My network.

Industrial design aspects of the Xiaomi Tag have drawn attention from technology enthusiasts comparing the product to other trackers on the market. Reports describe a minimalist form factor with rounded edges, a discrete Xiaomi logo and a small opening or accessory ring that allows the device to be fixed to straps or keyrings. According to design notes referenced in Chinese tech media, the tracker balances durability and light weight, with materials and color choices selected to blend into personal items without attracting undue attention while still being easy for owners to recognize.

Early pricing information shared by users who obtained Xiaomi marketing leaflets suggests that the standard Bluetooth Low Energy version of the Xiaomi Tag will be sold at 69 yuan per unit in the Chinese market. Retailers contacted by local media said this preliminary price point appears intended to position the tracker as an affordable accessory for everyday use, although final sales terms, including possible introductory promotions or bundled offers, have not yet been officially confirmed by the company.

The new Xiaomi Tag is described in preliminary materials as carrying an IP67 protection rating, offering resistance to dust and temporary immersion in water, according to information posted by technology bloggers this week. Commentators say such a rating would allow the device to withstand everyday exposure such as splashes or brief submersion, which is considered important for products routinely attached to items used outdoors. The certification details have yet to appear in an official regulatory filing, and some specifications could still be refined.

Alongside the standard listing, Xiaomi Mall presents a separate purchase option for the Xiaomi Tag UWB model, reflecting Xiaomi’s strategy to segment the product line by functionality and price. This variant is shown with a 129 yuan price in the Chinese market and is described as adding ultra-wideband capabilities to support more precise ranging, directional finding and improved location accuracy. According to product descriptions circulating on Chinese e-commerce platforms, the UWB version is designed to work closely with compatible Xiaomi smartphones and to enhance the responsiveness of the device-finding interface, especially in indoor environments.

Security and privacy considerations have also been raised in connection with the Xiaomi Tag, as industry norms now require protections against unwanted tracking. Commentators following Xiaomi’s launch in China have discussed how such devices should incorporate anti-stalking alerts, pairing confirmation and controls to prevent surreptitious use. Within this context, Xiaomi’s integration with its own device-finding ecosystem and the marketed support for Apple’s Find My network suggest that established safeguards, such as periodic alerts and device-identity management, will be important to consumer acceptance of both the Bluetooth and UWB models.

In recent reports from China, Xiaomi has publicly detailed the Xiaomi Tag product line, presenting it as part of its broader device-finding ecosystem. Promotional materials emphasize a compact tracker design with Xiaomi branding, simple contours and attachment points, while clearly separating the standard Bluetooth version, priced at 69 yuan, from the more advanced ultra-wideband (UWB) variant at 129 yuan. Key feature summaries refer to precise location tracking, ecosystem integration with Xiaomi phones and tablets, and marketing references to support for Apple’s Find My network as a differentiating highlight.

One image circulating among Chinese technology enthusiasts lists several Xiaomi smartphones as compatible with the ultra‑wideband locating feature of the Xiaomi Tag UWB model. According to the picture, the devices include the Xiaomi 17 Pro, Xiaomi 17 Pro Max and Xiaomi 17 Ultra, all described as supporting advanced directional guidance. Commentators note that the list appears to focus on higher‑end handsets and may be updated as newer models are released, with official compatibility information expected at launch.

Xiaomi’s upcoming tracking accessory is shown in promotional screenshots as being integrated into the company’s existing software ecosystem, including support for a Find Device function and dedicated controls on the HyperOS interface. According to descriptions by users who shared the images, owners will be able to view the approximate location of tagged items from within the operating system on compatible smartphones. Observers note that this approach echoes similar item‑finding services offered by other major handset manufacturers in the Chinese market.

Source posts circulating on technology forums in China indicate that Xiaomi plans to offer multi‑pack options for its forthcoming tracking tags alongside single‑unit sales. According to images of alleged price lists, a pack of four standard Xiaomi Tag units is marked at 239 yuan, while a set of four UWB units is listed at 396 yuan. Commentators note that these bundle prices, if confirmed, would provide a discount compared with individual purchases, though official marketing materials and final retail arrangements are still awaited.

According to early technical briefs and product images shared on Chinese social platforms, the Xiaomi Tag line is designed to operate using a CR2032 coin‑cell battery, a format widely available in retail channels. Industry analysts note that reliance on this common battery type may simplify replacement for users and reduce maintenance costs. Retailers say they have not yet received detailed guidance on replacement procedures or recommended accessory kits, which are expected to be clarified closer to the commercial launch.

Xiaomi is preparing to introduce a new line of compact tracking accessories under the product name Xiaomi Tag, according to promotional materials circulating in recent days on Chinese social media. The range, described by people familiar with the launch plans, includes a standard model and a separate Xiaomi Tag UWB variant aimed at users who require more advanced positioning capabilities. The devices are expected to be integrated into the company’s existing mobile ecosystem and will initially target consumers in mainland China, with further details on availability to be clarified by the manufacturer.

Xiaomi’s device-finding interface, demonstrated in app views shared on platforms such as Weibo, shows how users can add a new Xiaomi Tag, associate it with their account and assign custom names for different belongings. The interface typically guides owners through discovery of the tracker via Bluetooth, confirmation of ownership, and display of the tagged object’s last known position on a map. According to technical descriptions from Xiaomi, this system underpins both the standard 69 yuan tracker and the 129 yuan UWB variant, with mapping, sound alerts and guided locating playing central roles in the user experience.

Promotional descriptions circulating online state that the official rated battery life for Xiaomi’s forthcoming tracker could reach up to one year on a single CR2032 cell, depending on usage patterns. People familiar with the company’s planning stress that this figure is presented as a maximum under test conditions and that actual endurance may vary. The claimed runtime, if confirmed, would place the device broadly in line with competing personal trackers already sold in the Chinese consumer electronics market.

The UWB version of the Xiaomi Tag is being promoted as offering enhanced directional finding abilities when paired with certain Xiaomi smartphones, according to people who have reviewed the company’s preliminary promotional content. The materials suggest that, on supported handsets, users may be able to see more precise distance and direction indicators when searching for tagged items. Analysts caution that the described functions are likely to depend on hardware support in the phones themselves and may not be available across the entire product range.

Technical specifications shared in recent days by people who claim to have seen Xiaomi’s internal documentation describe the upcoming tracker as weighing around 10 grams and measuring approximately 7.2 millimeters in thickness. The compact form factor is portrayed as suitable for attachment to small personal belongings without adding significant bulk. Industry observers say these dimensions are broadly in line with similar consumer tracking devices already available in the Chinese electronics market.

Information currently available about the Xiaomi Tag, including pricing, specifications and software features, relates to a launch planned for the Chinese domestic market, according to people familiar with the company’s distribution strategy. Retailers and online platforms in mainland China are understood to be preparing for the product’s arrival in the near term. Observers note that there has been no formal indication yet of release plans for other regions, and any international expansion would likely be announced separately at a later date.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post text accurately reflects the evidence regarding the Xiaomi Tag's launch, pricing, specifications, and features without fabrication, distortion, or misleading context.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is a faithful, detailed summary of the available evidence regarding the Xiaomi Tag launch, maintaining accurate facts and tone.

image_analysis.global_notes: All images are clean and accurate representations of the Xiaomi Tag product, its pricing, features, and user interface, as described in the post text and evidence.

judge.level3_rationale: 该帖子准确无误地报道了小米Tag在中国市场的发布详情，包括价格、规格、功能及兼容性，所有核心主张均有证据支持，且配图均为真实的官方营销素材或电商截图，与文本内容完全一致，无任何失真或误导。
judge.reasoning_trace: All subclaims are supported by evidence; images are authentic and match the text; no distortions detected.
judge.core_claim: Xiaomi has launched the Xiaomi Tag in China, offering a standard Bluetooth version for 69 yuan and a UWB version for 129 yuan, with features like 10g weight, 7.2mm thickness, IP67 rating, and compatibility with Apple Find My.
key_subclaims: The standard Xiaomi Tag costs 69 yuan and the UWB version costs 129 yuan.
The device weighs 10 grams and is 7.2 millimeters thick.
The Xiaomi Tag has an IP67 dust and water resistance rating.
The UWB version supports Apple Find My compatibility.
The UWB version works with specific Xiaomi phones like the Xiaomi 17 Pro, 17 Pro Max, and 17 Ultra.
The standard version uses Bluetooth Low Energy for tracking.
The device uses a CR2032 coin-cell battery with up to one year of battery life.
The device can be attached to keys, bags, wallets, and luggage.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The standard Xiaomi Tag costs 69 yuan and the UWB version costs 129 yuan.
subclaim_findings.note: Social posts and product pages confirm these exact prices.

subclaim_findings.note: Technical specifications shared in briefs confirm these dimensions.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The Xiaomi Tag has an IP67 dust and water resistance rating.
subclaim_findings.note: Preliminary materials and product pages confirm the IP67 rating.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The UWB version supports Apple Find My compatibility.
subclaim_findings.note: Social posts explicitly mention compatibility with Apple Find My.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The UWB version works with specific Xiaomi phones like the Xiaomi 17 Pro, 17 Pro Max, and 17 Ultra.
subclaim_findings.note: Images circulating among tech enthusiasts list these specific models as compatible.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The standard version uses Bluetooth Low Energy for tracking.
subclaim_findings.note: Social posts and product descriptions confirm BLE usage for the standard model.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The device uses a CR2032 coin-cell battery with up to one year of battery life.
subclaim_findings.note: Technical briefs confirm the CR2032 battery and the one-year rated life.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The device can be attached to keys, bags, wallets, and luggage.
subclaim_findings.note: Social posts and marketing visuals show these exact use cases.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post text is a comprehensive and accurate summary of the Xiaomi Tag launch, with all specific claims (pricing, specs, features, compatibility) fully supported by the retrieved evidence. The images are authentic screenshots and marketing materials that directly correspond to the text, showing no signs of fabrication, editing, or contextual mismatch. No L2 labels are triggered.
